module.label: Pediatric urinary symptoms, UTI, or pyelonephritis

use_when: Child or adolescent with dysuria, urinary frequency, new wetting, malodorous/cloudy urine, hematuria, suspected UTI, febrile UTI, flank or loin pain, pyelonephritis concern, recurrent UTI, catheter/anomaly context, or fever without focus where UTI is clinically plausible.

Pediatric urinary workup needs age-band routing, urine collection method, dipstick/microscopy/culture interpretation, upper versus lower UTI triage, sepsis/infant escalation, imaging thresholds, STI and pregnancy-sensitive adolescent add-ons, and source-backed testing restraint.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult dysuria, pyelonephritis, AKI, renal colic, or sepsis pathway as appropriate.
Unstable child with shock, sepsis, altered mental status, meningitis concern, severe dehydration, or active resuscitation needs; escalate immediately while documenting focused urinary findings.

Primary abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, suspected appendicitis, bowel obstruction, ectopic pregnancy, or acute scrotum without urinary symptoms; use the pediatric abdominal/vomiting pathway and add this workup only when urinary features are present.
Known complex renal transplant, dialysis, oncology/neutropenia, or urologic post-operative state where local specialty protocol overrides this general pediatric UTI module.

modifier_add_on_policy.rationale: This validated source-specific pediatric UTI pack already includes infant/sepsis, upper-tract, urine-collection, catheter/anomaly, STI/pregnancy, and follow-up routing; generic adult infection modifier prompts should not be activated by the pack's own urinary aliases.

differentialBuckets.label: Lower UTI or cystitis pattern with dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, new wetting, suprapubic discomfort, malodorous/cloudy urine, or hematuria
differentialBuckets.action: Confirm urinary symptoms, collect urine before antibiotics when feasible, interpret dipstick/microscopy by age, send culture when indicated, and avoid treating asymptomatic bacteriuria.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Older children may present with classic lower urinary symptoms, but diagnosis still depends on urine testing and culture context rather than symptom labels alone.

source.source_section: symptoms/signs, urine collection, urine testing, and lower UTI treatment principles
source.implementation_notes: Use symptom/sign clusters to decide urine testing; no single symptom alone should determine testing or treatment.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Lower urinary tract infection, cystitis, urethritis mimic, contamination, or asymptomatic bacteriuria.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urine collection, culture, empiric antibiotic decision, symptom relief, follow-up for culture/sensitivities, and recurrence-prevention counseling.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad lower-UTI frame; NICE uses symptom/sign clusters plus urine testing and culture confirmation.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not diagnose UTI from malodorous urine, dipstick blood/protein, pyuria alone, or bag culture alone.

differentialBuckets.label: Upper UTI, pyelonephritis, or perinephric abscess pattern with fever, rigors, vomiting, malaise, loin/flank pain, renal-angle tenderness, or systemic illness
differentialBuckets.action: Screen systemic features and upper-tract symptoms; escalate, culture before antibiotics when possible, and add blood tests/imaging when pyelonephritis, abscess, sepsis, or poor response is suspected.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Upper UTI and pyelonephritis change acuity, antibiotic route, renal-function testing, blood culture, ultrasound, admission, and follow-up.

source.source_section: pyelonephritis/peri-nephric abscess and investigations
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric antimicrobial guidance; this row handles triage and testing consequences.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Acute upper UTI, pyelonephritis, perinephric abscess, bacteremia, or sepsis from urinary source.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes senior review, parenteral antibiotics, blood culture, FBC/renal function, renal ultrasound, admission, transfer, and culture follow-up.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad upper-UTI frame; systemic features and source guidance determine escalation.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Younger infants may have nonspecific presentations; absence of flank pain does not exclude upper UTI in infants.

differentialBuckets.label: Infant UTI, fever without focus, or sepsis-risk urinary source
differentialBuckets.action: Identify age band and illness severity first; refer babies under 3 months with suspected UTI, send urgent microscopy/culture, and use pediatric sepsis/febrile-infant pathways when physiology or age requires.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Age under 3 months and sepsis physiology materially change urine collection, antibiotic route, admission, lumbar-puncture consideration, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: babies under 3 months, serious illness risk assessment, and sepsis/neonatal infection routing
source.implementation_notes: Pair with pediatric fever/sepsis or febrile-infant pathway when fever or toxicity is present.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Febrile infant UTI, serious bacterial illness, urosepsis, neonatal infection, or meningitis co-infection concern.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes specialist referral, urgent urine microscopy/culture, blood culture/LP consideration, IV antibiotics, admission, transfer, and discharge eligibility.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this infant/sepsis frame; age and physiology determine management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A negative dipstick is less reliable in neonates and young infants; do not use it to dismiss an unwell infant.

differentialBuckets.label: Complex urinary tract, recurrent UTI, catheter/anomaly, STI, pregnancy, renal stone, vulvovaginitis/urethritis, appendicitis, or contamination mimic
differentialBuckets.action: Ask risk factors and mimics before treating; clarify collection method, prior cultures, anatomy/catheter/prophylaxis, adolescent sexual/pregnancy context, and alternative abdominal/genital causes.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Complex urinary context and mimics change culture interpretation, antimicrobial stewardship, imaging, specialty advice, and whether another validated workup should be added.

source.source_section: increased-risk children, STI screening, catheter/anomaly cautions, and urine collection contamination
source.implementation_notes: Do not treat pyuria or colonization alone in chronically catheterized children; seek specialty advice.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Recurrent UTI, atypical UTI, renal tract anomaly, catheter colonization, STI/urethritis, pregnancy-related mimic, renal stone, genital irritation, appendicitis, or contaminated specimen.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes culture collection/recollection, STI testing, pregnancy testing, imaging, antibiotic choice/route, pediatric/urology/ID advice, and whether abdominal/scrotal/pelvic workups are added.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this complex/mimic frame; source-defined risk and contamination rules drive next steps.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This general module does not replace transplant, dialysis, oncology, or local catheter/anomaly protocols.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure temperature, fever duration, antipyretic timing, and whether fever lacks another clear source.
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes urine-testing need, upper-UTI concern, sepsis screening, infant referral, blood tests, and admission decisions.

source.source_section: symptoms/signs and risk of serious illness
source.implementation_notes: Fever is baseline safety data, not a physical exam maneuver.

safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes urine testing, upper-UTI routing, pediatric fever/sepsis add-on, and discharge safety.
safetyChecks.limitations: Temperature alone does not diagnose UTI, pyelonephritis, or sepsis; interpret with symptoms and urine testing.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure with an age-appropriate cuff and repeat if high, low, painful, or inconsistent with perfusion.
safetyChecks.rationale: Blood pressure affects sepsis/shock recognition and can reveal hypertension or renal involvement in urinary presentations.

source.source_section: examination and blood pressure
source.implementation_notes: Interpret with age-specific norms and illness severity.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal blood pressure changes sepsis escalation, renal review, fluid safety, admission, and follow-up.
safetyChecks.limitations: Wrong cuff size, distress, movement, and pain can distort pediatric readings.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and interpret with age, fever, pain, hydration, and perfusion.
safetyChecks.rationale: Persistent tachycardia may reflect fever, pain, dehydration, sepsis, pyelonephritis, or need for senior review.
findings_options: tachycardia explained by fever or pain

source.source_section: toxic features and physiological triggers
source.implementation_notes: Heart rate is baseline acuity data; escalate when persistent or accompanied by poor perfusion.

safetyChecks.management_change: Persistent abnormal heart rate changes sepsis screening, fluids, antibiotics timing, monitoring, and transfer threshold.
safetyChecks.limitations: Fever, anxiety, pain, dehydration, medications, and crying affect heart rate.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Count respiratory rate for a full minute when feasible and correlate with fever, work of breathing, dehydration, and sepsis concern.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachypnea can be a sepsis or dehydration signal and can also point to an alternative fever source that lowers UTI likelihood.

source.source_section: symptoms/signs decreasing UTI likelihood and serious illness risk assessment
source.implementation_notes: Respiratory signs can suggest an alternative fever source while abnormal physiology still needs escalation.

safetyChecks.management_change: Respiratory abnormality changes sepsis screening, respiratory-source evaluation, and urine testing threshold.
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, fever, pain, and anxiety affect respiratory rate.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status
safetyChecks.action: Document alertness, irritability, lethargy, weak cry, responsiveness to caregiver, and change from baseline.
safetyChecks.rationale: Lethargy, weak cry, or altered responsiveness changes concern for sepsis, dehydration, meningitis, or unsafe discharge.

source.source_section: toxic features and age-dependent UTI history
source.implementation_notes: Mental status is baseline acuity data, not a physical exam maneuver.

safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal mental status changes sepsis pathway, blood culture/LP consideration, IV antibiotics, admission, and transfer.
safetyChecks.limitations: Sleep, fever, pain, fear, developmental baseline, and medications can complicate interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to mental status as baseline acuity data.

safetyChecks.label: Document intake and urine output
safetyChecks.action: Document fluid intake, vomiting, ability to tolerate oral antibiotics/fluids, wet nappies or urine output, last urine, and urinary retention symptoms.
safetyChecks.rationale: Intake and urine output change dehydration severity, urine-collection feasibility, oral versus IV route, admission, and safety-netting.
findings_options: fewer wet nappies or reduced urine
no urine or urinary retention concern
vomiting limits oral fluids or antibiotics
unable to obtain intake or urine history

source.source_section: history, treatment route, and discharge criteria
source.implementation_notes: Pair with hydration exam and current weight for medication/fluid planning.

safetyChecks.management_change: Poor intake, vomiting, or low urine output changes oral/IV antibiotics, fluids, admission, and follow-up.
safetyChecks.equipment_needed: caregiver report and intake/output record when available
safetyChecks.limitations: Caregiver estimates vary; use observed intake/output and exam when possible.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to intake/output as baseline safety data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Measure or verify current weight in kilograms before antibiotic, analgesic, fluid, renal-function, or transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric UTI and pyelonephritis treatment uses weight-based medication and fluid decisions; missing weight can cause dosing errors.

source.source_section: treatment age-dependence and empiric antibiotic route
source.implementation_notes: Use measured weight when feasible; do not delay emergency care solely for weight.

safetyChecks.management_change: Weight changes antibiotic and fluid dosing, renal dosing review, and handoff safety.
safetyChecks.limitations: Use institutional weight-estimation tools if urgent treatment cannot wait.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as medication-safety data.

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric urinary age band
requiredQuestions.text: Which age band applies: under 28 days, 29 days to under 3 months, 3 to 6 months, 6 months to under 3 years, 3 to 11 years, 12 to 15 years, or 16 to 21 years?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric urinary/UTI workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Age determines symptom specificity, dipstick reliability, urine collection method, referral, antibiotic route, imaging, and discharge safety.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Infants under 3 months require specialist referral/IV-route consideration; neonates require febrile-infant/neonatal infection routing.

source.source_section: babies under 3 months and age-based imaging tables
source.implementation_notes: Keep age band visible so adult GU logic is not applied to children.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to age band; it changes pathway selection and risk.

requiredQuestions.label: Classic urinary symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Are painful urination, more frequent urination, urgency, hesitancy, new daytime accidents or bedwetting, malodorous/cloudy/darker urine, visible hematuria, suprapubic pain, or urinary retention present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when urinary symptoms, fever without focus, abdominal pain, flank pain, or suspected UTI is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies urinary symptom clusters that increase UTI likelihood and justify urine collection/testing.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive urinary symptoms change urine testing, culture, lower versus upper UTI triage, STI/mimic add-ons, and treatment decisions.

source.source_section: symptoms and signs that increase UTI likelihood
source.implementation_notes: No single symptom alone should decide testing; use clusters and clinical judgement.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available from NICE for this symptom cluster; symptoms guide whether urine collection/testing is necessary.

requiredQuestions.label: Infant nonspecific UTI features
requiredQuestions.text: In an infant or pre-verbal child, are fever, vomiting, poor feeding, lethargy, irritability, failure to thrive or faltering growth, jaundice, abdominal pain, hematuria, or offensive urine present?
options: failure to thrive or faltering growth
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for infants, toddlers, pre-verbal children, and fever without focus.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Captures age-dependent nonspecific UTI features that are easy to miss in young children.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive infant features change urine testing, fever/sepsis routing, specialist referral, antibiotic route, and admission decisions.

source.source_section: age-dependent clinical features by age
source.implementation_notes: Use direct caregiver observation and avoid assuming absence of classic urinary symptoms rules out UTI in young children.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this nonspecific infant cluster; age-dependent features guide testing threshold.

requiredQuestions.label: Upper-tract and systemic features
requiredQuestions.text: Are fever, rigors or shivering, malaise, vomiting, loin/flank pain, renal-angle pain, abdominal pain, poor oral tolerance, or persistent symptoms despite treatment present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every suspected UTI because upper versus lower UTI changes acuity and tests.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pyelonephritis, perinephric abscess, bacteremia, or sepsis-risk presentation.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive upper-tract features change senior review, renal function/FBC/blood culture, renal ultrasound, IV route, admission, and follow-up.

source.source_section: assessment of severity and pyelonephritis features
source.implementation_notes: Younger children may not localize loin/flank pain; systemic features matter.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for upper-tract feature clusters; source guidance uses systemic and localizing features.

requiredQuestions.label: UTI risk factors and prior history
requiredQuestions.text: Is there previous confirmed UTI, previous imaging, recurrent UTI, known renal tract anomaly, abnormal urinary stream, high-grade vesicoureteral reflux, renal stones, urinary instrumentation, long-term catheter, prophylactic antibiotics, constipation, bladder/bowel dysfunction, spina bifida, or neurogenic bladder?
options: abnormal urinary stream or poor urine flow
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric urinary/UTI workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies recurrent, atypical, catheter-associated, anomaly-associated, or obstructive urinary risk that changes testing and follow-up.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive risk factors change culture interpretation, imaging, specialty advice, empiric antibiotic selection, recurrence prevention, and discharge plan.

source.source_section: history and risk factors
source.implementation_notes: Ask about prior culture organism and imaging when known.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to risk-factor review; risk factors determine complex pathway and follow-up.

requiredQuestions.label: Alternative fever source and urinary mimics
requiredQuestions.text: Is there a clear respiratory, ear, skin, abdominal, genital, or other fever source; nappy rash or vulvovaginal irritation; appendicitis-like pain; renal colic; trauma; medication exposure; or asymptomatic urine testing without urinary symptoms?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask before diagnosing UTI from nonspecific symptoms or abnormal urine screening.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Reduces overdiagnosis by identifying conditions that lower UTI likelihood or require another workup.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Alternative sources can defer urine testing in well children with clear source, trigger another validated workup, or prevent treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria.

source.source_section: symptoms/signs decreasing UTI likelihood and asymptomatic bacteriuria
source.implementation_notes: UTI testing is still appropriate when multiple UTI features, age risk, or illness severity persists.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; source guidance uses alternative-source features to inform testing decisions.

conditionalQuestions.label: Urine collection context
conditionalQuestions.text: Which urine collection method is feasible or already used: clean catch, midstream, catheter specimen, suprapubic aspirate, urine pad, bag specimen, or unknown method?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when urine has been collected, is planned, or culture interpretation is needed.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Collection method determines contamination risk and whether culture can confirm UTI.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Bag urine cannot diagnose UTI; catheter/SPA/clean-catch context changes culture interpretation, recollection, and antibiotics.

source.source_section: urine collection methods and contamination cautions
source.implementation_notes: Document collection method on pathology request when available.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to collection method; it determines culture validity and contamination risk.

conditionalQuestions.label: Adolescent STI and pregnancy context
conditionalQuestions.text: For an adolescent with urinary symptoms, is there sexual activity, genital discharge or lesions, pelvic pain, pregnancy possibility, postpartum state, estrogen therapy, or need for confidential STI/pregnancy testing?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when adolescent urinary symptoms could overlap STI, pelvic pain, pregnancy, or postpartum risk.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes UTI from STI/urethritis/PID/pregnancy-related or postpartum mimics and safety constraints.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes STI PCR testing, pregnancy testing, pelvic/OB add-on, imaging/medication safety, and discharge counseling.

source.source_section: sexually active children and STI screening
source.implementation_notes: Use local confidentiality and consent policy.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to adolescent STI/pregnancy context; it changes the differential and testing safety.

conditionalQuestions.label: Catheter, anomaly, or prophylaxis details
conditionalQuestions.text: If catheter/anomaly/prophylaxis context is present, what is the catheter type and duration, baseline colonization, recent instrumentation, known organism/resistance, renal tract anomaly, prophylactic antibiotic, or treating specialist plan?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when complex urinary anatomy, catheter, neurogenic bladder, or prophylaxis is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies cases where pyuria/bacteriuria may not equal symptomatic infection and where specialty advice is needed.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes empiric therapy, culture interpretation, catheter handling, ID/urology/pediatric advice, and antibiotic stewardship.

source.source_section: children with catheters, renal tract anomalies, and prophylactic antibiotics
source.implementation_notes: Do not exchange chronic catheters or treat colonization without local/specialty guidance.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to catheter/anomaly context; it changes interpretation and stewardship.

conditionalQuestions.label: Atypical or recurrent UTI features
conditionalQuestions.text: Are seriously ill appearance, poor urine flow, abdominal or bladder mass, raised creatinine, septicemia, failure to respond within 48 hours, non-E. coli organism, or recurrent UTI criteria present?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when imaging, recurrence, atypical infection, poor response, or specialist follow-up is being considered.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies NICE atypical and recurrent UTI categories that change imaging and follow-up.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change acute ultrasound, delayed ultrasound, DMSA scan, MCUG consideration, specialist follow-up, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: definitions of atypical and recurrent UTI and imaging recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Use age-specific imaging tables rather than routine imaging for every first UTI.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to atypical/recurrent classification; it is an imaging and follow-up trigger.

requiredExam.label: Palpate suprapubic area
requiredExam.technique: Palpate gently over the suprapubic area for tenderness, fullness, or bladder distension.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when urinary symptoms, suspected lower UTI, retention, abdominal pain, or bladder mass concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Cystitis, bladder distension, urinary retention, abdominal/bladder mass, or lower abdominal mimic.

source.source_section: examination and lower abdominal or loin tenderness
source.implementation_notes: This is a focused maneuver; do not substitute a generic abdominal exam label.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; findings guide lower UTI versus retention/mimic evaluation.
requiredExam.management_change: Suprapubic tenderness or fullness changes urine testing, retention evaluation, imaging consideration, and add-on abdominal workup.
requiredExam.limitations: Pain, anxiety, full bladder, constipation, and body habitus affect interpretation.

requiredExam.label: Palpate loin tenderness
requiredExam.technique: Palpate or gently percuss each renal angle/loin region only as tolerated, comparing sides and stopping if painful.
findings_options: exam limited by pain or age
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, rigors, vomiting, flank/loin pain, abdominal pain, pyelonephritis, renal stone, or upper-tract concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Pyelonephritis, renal-angle tenderness, perinephric abscess, renal colic, or upper urinary tract involvement.

source.source_section: pyelonephritis/peri-nephric abscess features and examination
source.implementation_notes: Young children may not localize; absence of tenderness does not rule out upper UTI.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; upper-tract symptoms plus tenderness change acuity and testing.
requiredExam.management_change: Loin tenderness changes pyelonephritis/abscess testing, blood culture/renal function, ultrasound, antibiotics route, and admission.
requiredExam.limitations: Flank tenderness is absent or hard to assess in many infants and young children.

requiredExam.label: Inspect oral mucosa
requiredExam.technique: Inspect lips, tongue, oral mucosa, tears, and ability to tolerate oral fluids while correlating with intake and urine output.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, vomiting, poor intake, reduced urine output, pyelonephritis, or discharge safety is being assessed.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Dehydration, oral intolerance, sepsis risk, and need for IV/enteral fluids or admission.

source.source_section: toxic features, dehydration, and disposition
source.implementation_notes: Hydration inspection is an exam maneuver; intake/output and vitals remain baseline safety data.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; hydration findings change acuity and route decisions.
requiredExam.management_change: Dehydration or oral intolerance changes fluids, IV antibiotics, admission, and safety-netting.
requiredExam.limitations: Mouth breathing, crying, fever, and recent drinks affect mucosa assessment.

requiredExam.label: Inspect external genitalia when indicated
requiredExam.technique: With consent, chaperone/privacy, and developmentally appropriate explanation, inspect only when clinically indicated for irritation, discharge, lesions, labial adhesions, phimosis, trauma, or collection barriers.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform only when urinary symptoms, collection difficulty, genital irritation, discharge/lesion, STI concern, trauma, or anatomic concern makes it clinically necessary.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Genital irritation, vulvovaginitis, urethritis/STI, labial adhesion, phimosis, trauma, safeguarding concern, or urine-collection barrier.

source.source_section: examination and increased-risk genitourinary conditions
source.implementation_notes: Use local consent, chaperone, child-protection, and adolescent confidentiality policies.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; findings change mimic, STI, anatomic, and safeguarding pathways.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal findings change STI testing, urine-collection method, safeguarding route, specialty advice, and whether UTI is the primary diagnosis.
requiredExam.equipment_needed: privacy, consent, and chaperone per local policy
requiredExam.limitations: Do not perform unnecessary genital exams; defer or escalate when consent, distress, or safeguarding concerns require specialist handling.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate abdomen for bladder or abdominal mass
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate gently for bladder distension, abdominal mass, or focal abdominal tenderness when retention, atypical UTI, obstruction, or abdominal mimic is possible.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when poor urine flow, retention, mass, obstruction, recurrent/atypical UTI, or abdominal mimic is possible.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Urinary retention, obstructive uropathy, abdominal/bladder mass, constipation, or abdominal mimic.

source.source_section: atypical UTI features including poor urine flow and abdominal or bladder mass
source.implementation_notes: Use as an individual maneuver, not a generic abdominal exam.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; mass or obstruction features trigger imaging/specialty review.
conditionalExam.management_change: Mass, retention, or obstruction changes ultrasound urgency, renal function testing, catheter/specialty advice, and disposition.
conditionalExam.limitations: Constipation, body habitus, pain, and bladder filling can affect palpation.

conditionalExam.label: Test lower-limb reflex symmetry
conditionalExam.technique: Test patellar or ankle reflex symmetry when bladder dysfunction or spinal cause is suspected, and add a validated neuro/spine workup if abnormal or if weakness/saddle symptoms are present.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when urinary retention, bladder/bowel dysfunction, spina bifida, neurogenic bladder, leg symptoms, or spinal red flags are present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Neurogenic bladder, spinal dysraphism, cord/cauda equina process, or bladder emptying impairment.

source.source_section: examination for lower limb neurological findings in impaired bladder emptying
source.implementation_notes: Add pediatric neuro/spine pathway when neurologic red flags are present.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; neurologic findings change the workup outside simple UTI.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal findings change spine/neuro evaluation, urinary retention management, imaging, and specialty referral.
conditionalExam.limitations: This is not routine for every UTI; perform only when bladder dysfunction or neurologic clues are present.

redFlags.label: Suspected UTI in baby under 3 months
redFlags.action: Refer babies under 3 months with suspected UTI to pediatric specialist care; send urgent urine microscopy/culture and use febrile-infant/neonatal infection routing when fever or toxicity is present.
redFlags.rationale: Infants under 3 months have higher risk of serious bacterial infection, bacteremia, sepsis, and admission/IV antibiotic needs.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Infant UTI, febrile infant serious bacterial infection, neonatal infection, or sepsis.

source.source_section: babies under 3 months with suspected UTI
source.implementation_notes: Do not use older-child outpatient logic in this age band.

redFlags.management_change: Changes urgent referral, microscopy/culture, IV antibiotics, blood/CSF evaluation, admission, and discharge criteria.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to age-based escalation; age changes safety pathway.
redFlags.limitations: Local neonatal and febrile-infant protocols may be more specific and should be followed.

redFlags.label: Toxic appearance or sepsis physiology
redFlags.action: Escalate immediately for suspected sepsis, shock, altered mental status, weak cry, respiratory distress, moderate/severe dehydration, persistent tachycardia, hypotension, or poor perfusion.
redFlags.rationale: Sepsis physiology changes UTI care from outpatient testing to urgent resuscitation, parenteral antibiotics, cultures, fluids, and transfer planning.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Urosepsis, septic shock, severe dehydration, meningitis co-infection, or critical illness.

source.source_section: toxic features, sepsis pathway, and critical escalation
source.implementation_notes: Do not delay antibiotics for urine collection in suspected sepsis.

redFlags.management_change: Changes sepsis pathway, parenteral antibiotics, IV fluids, blood culture, LP consideration in neonates, admission, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to sepsis escalation; physiologic danger signs determine urgency.
redFlags.limitations: Clinical deterioration can be rapid; repeat vitals and escalate if trajectory worsens.

redFlags.label: Upper UTI or pyelonephritis features
redFlags.action: Escalate for upper-tract concern when fever/rigors, vomiting, malaise, flank/loin pain, renal-angle tenderness, abdominal pain, or poor oral tolerance is present.
redFlags.rationale: Upper UTI changes testing, antibiotic route/duration, imaging, admission, and follow-up compared with lower UTI.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Pyelonephritis, perinephric abscess, bacteremia, or upper urinary tract infection.

source.source_section: assessment of severity and pyelonephritis treatment
source.implementation_notes: Oral therapy may be appropriate in selected non-vomiting children, but upper tract features still require review and follow-up.

redFlags.management_change: Changes renal function/FBC/blood culture, ultrasound, antibiotic duration/route, observation, and admission threshold.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to upper-tract escalation; source-defined systemic and localizing features drive action.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of localizing loin pain does not exclude pyelonephritis in infants.

redFlags.label: Atypical or recurrent UTI
redFlags.action: Escalate imaging/follow-up planning when seriously ill appearance, poor urine flow, abdominal/bladder mass, raised creatinine, septicemia, poor 48-hour response, non-E. coli organism, or recurrent UTI is present.
redFlags.rationale: Atypical and recurrent UTI are source-defined triggers for ultrasound, DMSA/MCUG consideration, specialist follow-up, and reassessment.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Atypical UTI, recurrent UTI, obstruction, vesicoureteral reflux, renal scarring risk, or structural urinary anomaly.

source.source_section: definitions of atypical/recurrent UTI and imaging schedule
source.implementation_notes: Imaging is not routine for every first UTI; apply age and atypical/recurrent criteria.

redFlags.management_change: Changes acute ultrasound, delayed ultrasound, DMSA, MCUG consideration, specialist follow-up, and discharge safety.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to atypical/recurrent classification; it is a guideline-defined imaging trigger.
redFlags.limitations: Local imaging availability and specialty thresholds may vary.

redFlags.label: Contaminated sample, bag culture, chronic catheter, or colonization risk
redFlags.action: Do not treat a bag culture, mixed growth, pyuria alone, or chronic catheter colonization as definitive UTI without clinical context and appropriate recollection or specialty advice.
redFlags.rationale: False-positive and colonization-driven diagnoses can lead to unnecessary antibiotics, missed mimics, resistance, and loss of trust.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Contaminated specimen, asymptomatic bacteriuria, catheter colonization, or misdiagnosed UTI.

source.source_section: bag specimens, mixed growth, catheter cautions, and definitive diagnosis
source.implementation_notes: Bag specimens cannot be used for UTI diagnosis; chronic catheters require case-by-case advice.

redFlags.management_change: Changes recollection, culture interpretation, antibiotic stewardship, ID/urology advice, and whether another diagnosis should be pursued.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to contamination/colonization caution; specimen validity determines reliability.
redFlags.limitations: Do not delay sepsis treatment in an unstable child while resolving contamination questions.

initialTests.label: Urine sample before antibiotics when feasible
initialTests.action: Collect urine before antibiotics when possible using clean catch/midstream when feasible, catheter/SPA when clinically required, and avoid bag specimens for culture diagnosis.
initialTests.rationale: Appropriate urine collection is the foundation of pediatric UTI diagnosis and prevents false positives, missed pathogens, and unreliable culture follow-up.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Valid urine sample for dipstick, microscopy, and culture before treatment alters results.

source.source_section: urine collection and antibiotics timing
source.implementation_notes: Do not delay treatment in high-risk serious illness if urine cannot be obtained.

initialTests.management_change: Collection method changes culture validity, empiric treatment, recollection, and follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to collection method; specimen validity determines whether urine results can guide management.
initialTests.limitations: High-risk serious illness may require antibiotics before urine collection if collection would delay treatment.

initialTests.label: Dipstick microscopy and culture interpretation
initialTests.action: Use dipstick as screening in older children, send microscopy/culture when indicated, and require culture context to confirm UTI; recognize that nitrite absence, blood/protein, or pyuria alone is insufficient.
initialTests.rationale: Dipstick and microscopy guide initial management, but culture confirmation and specimen quality prevent overdiagnosis.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Confirmed UTI versus probable/possible UTI, contamination, asymptomatic bacteriuria, or mimic.

source.source_section: dipstick, microscopy, culture interpretation, and additional notes
source.implementation_notes: Culture results should be checked at 24 to 48 hours to confirm or adjust management.

initialTests.management_change: Positive dipstick/microscopy changes empiric treatment and culture follow-up; contamination or negative/discordant results change recollection and differential.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Guidelines do not provide one universal LR for pediatric dipstick/culture selection; age, collection method, leukocytes/nitrites, microscopy, and culture determine reliability.
initialTests.limitations: Dipstick is less reliable in neonates/young infants; false negatives occur and nitrites are organism/bladder-dwell-time dependent.

initialTests.label: Upper-tract labs and renal ultrasound when indicated
initialTests.action: When pyelonephritis, perinephric abscess, sepsis, upper renal tract features, raised creatinine, poor response, recurrent UTI, or atypical UTI is present, obtain source-directed labs and renal tract ultrasound per local pathway.
initialTests.rationale: Routine bloods/ultrasound are not needed for every UTI, but upper-tract, atypical, or recurrent features change testing and disposition.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pyelonephritis, perinephric abscess, obstruction, renal impairment, bacteremia, recurrent/atypical UTI, or structural urinary anomaly.

source.source_section: other investigations, pyelonephritis/peri-nephric abscess, and imaging
source.implementation_notes: CHQ recommends FBC, renal function, blood culture and renal ultrasound when pyelonephritis/perinephric abscess is suspected.

initialTests.management_change: Abnormal labs or ultrasound change antibiotics route/duration, admission, drainage/specialty review, renal follow-up, and transfer.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to the imaging/lab bundle; source-defined upper-tract and atypical criteria determine selection.
initialTests.limitations: A normal early ultrasound does not exclude uncomplicated pyelonephritis; use clinical trajectory and culture response.

initialTests.label: Age-based UTI imaging schedule
initialTests.action: Apply NICE age-based imaging: acute ultrasound for atypical UTI, ultrasound within 6 weeks for babies under 6 months with first UTI that responds, ultrasound for lower UTI only when under 6 months or recurrent, and DMSA/MCUG only under specified criteria.
initialTests.rationale: Imaging stewardship prevents both missed structural disease in high-risk children and unnecessary imaging after uncomplicated first UTI.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Structural urinary tract abnormality, obstruction, renal parenchymal defect, vesicoureteral reflux, or renal scarring risk.

source.source_section: imaging recommendations and tables 4 to 6
source.implementation_notes: Do not routinely image children over 6 months with first UTI responding to treatment unless atypical/recurrent criteria apply.

initialTests.management_change: Imaging results change urology/nephrology referral, MCUG/DMSA planning, prophylaxis consideration, recurrence counseling, and follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to guideline imaging schedules; imaging is selected by age and atypical/recurrent criteria.
initialTests.limitations: Local radiology and specialty pathways may refine timing; do not use routine imaging as a substitute for culture follow-up.

initialTests.label: Adolescent STI and pregnancy tests when indicated
initialTests.action: When adolescent sexual/pregnancy context is possible, add confidential pregnancy testing and first-pass urine NAAT/PCR for gonorrhea/chlamydia or local STI testing as appropriate.
initialTests.rationale: STIs and pregnancy-related conditions can mimic UTI and change antibiotics, imaging, counseling, safeguarding, and follow-up.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pregnancy, STI/urethritis/cervicitis/PID mimic, postpartum urinary concern, or medication/imaging safety constraint.

source.source_section: sexually active children and STI screening
source.implementation_notes: Use confidential adolescent care and local consent/safeguarding policy.

initialTests.management_change: Positive pregnancy/STI tests change diagnosis, treatment, partner/public-health follow-up, OB/GYN or STI pathway, and discharge advice.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to adolescent context testing; tests are selected because results materially change management.
initialTests.limitations: A UTI and STI/pregnancy-related condition can coexist; do not stop source evaluation prematurely.

dispositionRules.label: Infant or sepsis escalation
dispositionRules.action: Admit or obtain urgent pediatric/senior advice for infants under 3 months, neonates with fever, suspected sepsis, shock, toxic appearance, altered mental status, severe dehydration, meningitis concern, or need for IV antibiotics.
dispositionRules.rationale: These features are not safe for routine outpatient UTI management.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Infant UTI, neonatal infection, urosepsis, meningitis co-infection, or severe dehydration.

source.source_section: management, escalation, admission, and discharge
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric critical care/retrieval and sepsis pathways when unstable.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes urgent consultation, admission, transfer, IV antibiotics, cultures, fluids, LP consideration, and discharge eligibility.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to disposition escalation; age and physiology determine safety.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not delay stabilizing care while completing urinary source documentation.

dispositionRules.label: Uncomplicated outpatient UTI route
dispositionRules.action: For relatively well children age 3 months or older who tolerate oral fluids/antibiotics and lack sepsis, upper-tract, complex, atypical, or failed-treatment features, use oral therapy per local guideline with 24- to 48-hour culture review and safety net.
dispositionRules.rationale: Most uncomplicated pediatric UTIs can be managed without broad labs, IV antibiotics, admission, or routine imaging when follow-up is reliable.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Uncomplicated lower UTI/cystitis in a stable child.

source.source_section: treatment, follow-up, consultation, transfer, and discharge
source.implementation_notes: Culture review should confirm or adjust management; local antimicrobial guidance controls drug choice.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes from broad admission/testing to oral therapy, GP/clinical follow-up, culture check, recurrence prevention, and return precautions.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to outpatient disposition; safety criteria and culture follow-up determine route.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not apply if oral intolerance, unreliable follow-up, upper-tract features, infant age, or complex anatomy/catheter context is present.

dispositionRules.label: Upper or complex UTI route
dispositionRules.action: Seek senior/pediatric advice for upper renal tract features, pyelonephritis/perinephric abscess concern, renal tract anomalies, long-term catheter, prophylactic antibiotics, poor 48-hour response, atypical organism, or recurrent UTI.
dispositionRules.rationale: These contexts change the reliability of simple cystitis treatment and often require imaging, labs, specialty advice, or admission.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Upper UTI, abscess, recurrent/atypical UTI, renal anomaly, catheter-associated infection, resistant organism, or failed outpatient treatment.

source.source_section: non-toxic children needing senior advice, special considerations, and admission
source.implementation_notes: Antibiotic decisions in catheter/anomaly/prophylaxis cases should be case-by-case with relevant specialists.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes culture interpretation, empiric therapy, renal ultrasound/labs, admission, urology/ID/pediatrics involvement, and follow-up.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to complex-route disposition; guideline-defined risk features determine ownership.
dispositionRules.limitations: This module is not a transplant/dialysis/oncology protocol.

dispositionRules.label: Imaging and follow-up stewardship
dispositionRules.action: Avoid routine ultrasound/prophylaxis after an uncomplicated first UTI; arrange imaging/follow-up for under-6-month, recurrent, atypical, upper-tract, renal-anomaly, or poor-response cases per age-specific guidance.
dispositionRules.rationale: Clinician adoption requires clear thresholds: under-testing misses structural disease, while routine imaging/prophylaxis creates low-value care.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Structural urinary disease, reflux/scarring risk, recurrence prevention, and unnecessary imaging/prophylaxis avoidance.

source.source_section: preventing recurrence, prophylaxis, and imaging recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Pair imaging recommendations with culture follow-up and recurrence-prevention counseling.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes ultrasound/DMSA/MCUG planning, prophylaxis avoidance, specialty referral, recurrence prevention, and parent/caregiver education.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to imaging stewardship; criteria are guideline-defined.
dispositionRules.limitations: Local specialty protocols may alter imaging after abnormal antenatal scans or known renal disease.

dispositionRules.label: Culture follow-up, return precautions, and recurrence prevention
dispositionRules.action: Document culture follow-up within 24 to 48 hours, reassessment if not improving, urgent return for fever/toxic signs/vomiting/flank pain/poor intake/no urine, and prevention counseling for hydration, constipation, and not delaying voiding.
dispositionRules.rationale: UTI management is unsafe without culture review and clear return precautions because resistance, contamination, upper-tract disease, and deterioration can emerge after discharge.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Culture-confirmed UTI, resistance, failed outpatient treatment, recurrent UTI, dehydration, or upper-tract progression.

source.source_section: requirements for non-toxic children, discharge, follow-up, and caregiver education
source.implementation_notes: Use local follow-up ownership and antimicrobial stewardship processes.

dispositionRules.management_change: Changes follow-up ownership, antibiotic adjustment, urgent reassessment, recurrence prevention, and handoff quality.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to safety-net instructions; culture and clinical trajectory determine next action.
dispositionRules.limitations: Safety-netting does not replace admission when discharge criteria are not met.
